questions.question: Explain the order of constructor execution in a simple Java class hierarchy involving a single parent class and a child class. Provide a brief code example to illustrate your explanation.
keyPoints: The parent class constructor (super()) is called implicitly before the child class constructor body executes.
If the parent class has a no-argument constructor, it is called automatically. If it does not, the child class must explicitly call super(...) with the correct arguments.
The complete order is: 1) Parent class field initialization and constructor execution, 2) Child class field initialization, 3) Child class constructor body execution.

questions.question: Describe the role and execution order of static initialization blocks in relation to constructors when a class is first loaded and then instantiated.
keyPoints: Static blocks execute only once, when the class is first loaded by the JVM, which happens before any instance of the class is created.
The order of execution for a new object is: 1) Static initialization blocks (in order of appearance), 2) Instance initialization blocks, 3) The constructor.
Static blocks are used to initialize static fields and are not tied to object creation.

questions.question: Analyze a complex Java inheritance scenario involving multiple levels of inheritance, instance initialization blocks, and an explicit call to super(). Trace and explain the complete, precise order of execution for all steps when creating an instance of the most-derived class.
keyPoints: Execution begins with the static initialization of the most base class, then moves down the hierarchy to the most-derived class, following the class loading order.

For object creation: The constructor chain starts at the top (Object) and works down. Before any constructor body runs, all instance variable initializers and instance initialization blocks for that class are executed.

An explicit call to super(...) in a constructor must be the first statement and dictates which parent constructor is called, affecting the entire chain. The full path must be traced from the top-level parent down to the specific constructor being invoked for the instantiated object.
